statement: The EU's Common Security and Defence Policy (CSDP) operates alongside but does not replace NATO. Article 42(2) TEU states that EU defence policy 'shall not prejudice the specific character of the security and defence policy' of NATO members. Iceland is a founding NATO member (1949) but has no standing military, relying instead on the Icelandic Coast Guard and bilateral defence agreements. EU membership would involve participation in CSDP missions and the European Defence Agency, but would not require establishing armed forces. Six EU members (Austria, Cyprus, Finland, Ireland, Malta, Sweden — though Finland and Sweden joined NATO in 2023–2024) have historically maintained varying forms of military non-alignment.
source_name: Treaty on European Union, Article 42; NATO Treaty, Article 5
caveats: The EU's defence ambitions are evolving rapidly following Russia's invasion of Ukraine in 2022. The European Commission proposed a €150 billion defence spending plan in March 2025. Iceland's lack of a military is unique among NATO members and would create an unusual position within CSDP structures.

related_entries.statement: Sjálfstæðisflokkurinn (Independence Party) introduced a bill in Althingi proposing an 18-month cooling-off period that would prevent senior officials and ministerial advisors involved in negotiations with international organisations from accepting positions with those organisations after leaving office. The bill was introduced in the context of potential EU accession negotiations, framed as a safeguard against conflicts of interest. Similar 'revolving door' regulations exist at the EU institutional level: EU Commissioners face an 18-month cooling-off period (extended to 24 months for the Commission President), and the European Ombudsman has called for stricter enforcement. Several EU member states, including France and Germany, have analogous rules for senior civil servants.

Iceland's 2009 EU membership application was submitted in the aftermath of the 2008 financial crisis, when the collapse of Iceland's three largest banks led to a severe economic crisis. Public support for EU membership peaked at approximately 60% in early 2009 but declined as the economy recovered. The application was widely seen as a crisis-driven decision.

citations.report_title: Trump sagður beita sér í ESB-umræðunni hér á landi

claims.claim_text: ESB-aðild Íslands myndi koma niður á varnarmálum Bandaríkjanna.
